high_end: {name} profiles as a legitimate early contributor if the mental side keeps pace with the tools.
{name} has the kind of profile that will keep decision makers in the room longer than planned.
{name} looks like one of the cleaner bets in this class when the role is kept specific.
{name} has starter-level tape and enough physical upside to keep the grade near the top of the board.
{name} brings a premium blend of baseline skill and projectable traits.
{name} checks enough boxes that the debate is more about ceiling than roster fit.
{name} has the kind of profile staffs can sell themselves on early.
{name} looks ready to help quickly, though the final grade still depends on how the staff weighs the flaws.
{name} has enough bankable football skill that the discussion can move from whether he belongs to how soon he starts.
{name} gives the room a rare mix of immediate usefulness and long-term upside, even if the final stack is not automatic.
starter_path: {name} has a realistic starter path, but the landing spot and development plan matter.
{name} is not a finished product, though the role fit is easy to see.
{name} brings enough usable traits to project beyond pure depth.

developmental: {name} is more projection than polish right now.
{name} has traits worth developing, but the tape leaves real questions.
{name} needs time before the staff can trust him in a large role.
{name} is a traits bet with enough flaws to require a deliberate plan.
{name} flashes the outline of an NFL role without sustaining it snap to snap.
{name} is the kind of prospect who needs a staff that already knows what it wants from him.
{name} has tools that will keep scouts interested, but the week-one role should be limited.
{name} has enough talent to stick in a building while the details catch up.
{name} is a developmental swing with enough real traits to keep the room from dismissing the upside.

roster_bubble: {name} is a camp-and-depth profile unless one trait translates faster than expected.
{name} has to win on the margins to stick.
{name} looks like a late board player with a narrow early role.
{name} needs special teams, practice reps, or a niche package to create staying power.

{name} looks more like a roster competition player than a plug-in answer.

round_1: Projected as an early pick, but not immune to a miss if the weaknesses are real.
First-round consideration with enough volatility to split scouting rooms.
High board grade with starter expectations, though the floor is not as tidy as the surface profile.
Top-tier draft slot is justified if the staff believes the translatable traits are stable.
Should be in early-pick conversations, with the main debate centered on how quickly he can handle a full role.
The grade belongs near the top of the class, but the evaluation still needs a clear answer for the miss case.
round_2_3: Day-two value with a chance to outplay the slot if the role is protected early.
Could become a quality starter, but the bust path is visible.
Best treated as a good prospect with specific development needs, not a finished answer.
Fits the day-two range because the strengths are real and the holes are still coachable.
Can justify a meaningful investment if the staff has a defined usage plan.
The grade makes sense in the second or third round, especially for a team comfortable living with early variance.
round_4_5: Mid-round target who needs patience and a clear role.
Developmental player with enough traits to bet on, but no guarantee he survives early mistakes.
Useful board value if the staff is comfortable with the risk.
Best viewed as a traits-and-fit selection rather than a safe depth add.
Could carve out a role by year two if the staff can simplify the first assignment.
The mid-round case depends on whether one carrying trait is strong enough to dress on game day.
round_6_7: Late-round projection with special teams or package value as the first foothold.
Needs a specific staff fit to become more than depth.
Worth a late look, but the path to a second contract is narrow.
Draftable if the staff has a clear developmental lane and patience with the rough edges.
The late-round value is tied more to role clarity than complete tape.
A good camp could change the trajectory, but he is not a low-maintenance projection.
leftover: Priority free-agent type unless a team has a strong private grade.
Back-end roster profile with one or two traits to investigate.
Likely undrafted unless the medical, workout, or interview process changes the board.
More likely to enter as a camp competition player than a protected roster piece.
The free-agent case is built around fit, persistence, and whether a staff believes the best trait travels.
Needs the right depth chart and a strong summer to become more than a practice-squad conversation.

Low: The evaluation has a fairly stable floor for the projected role.
There are weaknesses, but the basic player profile is coherent.
The role translation looks cleaner than most players in this range.
The staff should have a good idea of what it is getting if the assignment stays reasonable.
The risk is more about ceiling than whether he belongs in the league.
Nothing in the report requires the team to invent a role from scratch.
Medium: The report carries some volatility because the good and bad traits both show up clearly.
The player could beat this grade, but there is enough uncertainty to avoid treating him as safe.
The staff fit will heavily influence whether the strengths matter on Sundays.
He can become a winning piece, but the developmental checkpoints need to be real.
There is enough variance here that two teams could stack him a full tier apart.
The projection works if the staff accepts the tradeoffs instead of pretending they are minor.
High: This is a boom-bust evaluation and the board grade could be wrong by a full tier.
The miss case is real: the traits may not overcome the football concerns.
A scout can like the tools and still worry the player never earns a stable role.
The gap between the best reps and the down-to-down floor is large enough to create real bust risk.
This is the kind of profile that can tempt a team early and frustrate the staff later.
The evaluation needs a strong conviction check before the team spends premium draft capital.

role_fit: The first NFL role should probably be {role}.
He should be evaluated first through the {role} lens.
The cleanest path starts with {role} duties before the staff expands the menu.
The board grade makes the most sense if {role} is the planned job.
He is easiest to project when the early job description stays close to {role}.
A staff should resist moving him away from the {role} role too quickly.
His best football probably comes when the scheme lets him live in the {role} role first.

default: Early usage should be narrow enough to let the best trait show up before the whole assignment expands.
The first season should be built around packages, special teams, and practice growth.
He should not be asked to solve every situation immediately.
The best early plan is to give him a defined job and let the weekly trust grow from there.
A patient staff can let the role expand once the low-end reps become less costly.
pocket_qb: Early usage should lean on rhythm throws, protected looks, and answers that arrive on time.
The staff should build the passing menu from concepts he can identify before the snap.
He needs a clean weekly plan that keeps protection, timing, and field vision connected.
scrambling_qb: Early usage should include movement throws and selective designed runs without turning every dropback into an escape drill.
The staff should use the legs as a pressure valve while demanding steady pocket answers.
His first role needs structure, because pure playmaking can hide issues until the league adjusts.
power_rb: Early usage should let him press downhill and finish runs without needing to manufacture every yard on his own.
He fits best in a rotation where contact balance and ball security matter more than pure space creation.
The staff can build trust with short-yardage, four-minute, and physical early-down work.
elusive_rb: Early usage should get him into space and avoid asking him to live only between tight interior gaps.
He can earn touches quickly if the staff creates clean angles through screens, draws, and spread looks.
The role works best when he can stress linebackers laterally before contact arrives.
boundary_wr: Early usage should give him defined outside releases and chances to win vertically or at the boundary.
He should be eased into a full route tree while the staff tests which releases travel against NFL corners.
The offense should let him work where size, stride length, and ball skills can matter.
slot_wr: Early usage should emphasize option routes, space work, and quick-game timing.
He can help sooner if the staff uses motion, stacks, and inside access to keep him clean.
The role should test route detail and hands before asking him to live outside.
inline_te: Early usage should keep him attached enough to develop blocking value while giving him simple passing-game answers.
The best plan is to let him win with alignment versatility without overloading the route menu.
He needs enough blocking trust to stay on the field before the receiving role can grow.
move_te: Early usage should use detached alignments, crossers, seams, and matchups against linebackers.
He can contribute sooner if the staff treats him as a space player first and a full-service tight end later.
The role needs receiving stress points while the blocking detail catches up.
pass_protecting_ot: Early usage should protect him from excessive assignment changes while the pass set becomes repeatable.
The staff should test him against speed and counters before assuming he can live alone on the edge.
He needs stable technique work before the offense leans on him as an island protector.
interior_run_blocker: Early usage should lean on double teams, angles, and downhill concepts that let his power show.
The staff should avoid making him survive only in space until the footwork becomes more consistent.
He fits best when the run game lets him create movement before asking him to redirect late.
speed_edge: Early usage should let him rush from wide alignments and build counters after tackles respect the arc.
The first pass-rush package should showcase burst without asking him to anchor every early down.
The staff can use him as a pressure specialist while the rush plan and run fits mature.
power_edge: Early usage should lean on edge-setting, pocket compression, and physical downs before widening the rush menu.
He fits best when the staff values heavy hands and run-game reliability as part of the first role.
The pass-rush plan should grow out of power before asking him to win as a pure speed player.
interior_rusher: Early usage should put him in attack alignments where first-step quickness and leverage can show up.
He can earn pressure-package snaps before he is ready for every run fit.
The staff should give him chances to penetrate rather than only occupy blocks.
nose_run_stopping_dt: Early usage should center on early downs, short-yardage, and keeping linebackers clean.
He can have value quickly if the staff accepts that the box-score production may be modest.
The role works best when block control and gap discipline are treated as the primary job.
coverage_lb: Early usage should simplify the run fits enough to keep him clean in coverage and pursuit.
He can help in sub packages if the staff protects him from constant downhill traffic.
The plan should let speed and space instincts show while the box responsibilities grow.
box_lb: Early usage should keep him near the action and let him trigger downhill.
He fits best in a role that values contact, run fits, and limited coverage stress early.
The staff should build the passing-down role only after the run-game trust is established.
man_cb: Early usage should test press and mirror ability without leaving him alone against every route type.
The staff should build the plan around leverage rules and matchups that suit his recovery profile.
He can play early if the technique holds up enough to survive isolation snaps.
zone_cb: Early usage should emphasize vision, route handoffs, and closing speed from zone spacing.
He fits best in a coverage plan that lets him read the quarterback and drive on throws.
The staff should avoid forcing a pure man role until the footwork proves it can hold.
deep_safety: Early usage should keep his eyes clean and let range, angles, and communication settle the back end.
He can earn trust if the staff sees enough discipline to limit explosive mistakes.
The role should grow from landmark consistency before adding heavier disguise responsibility.
box_safety: Early usage should put him near the line, in robber looks, and around the run fit.
He fits best when the staff lets physicality and short-area trigger show before asking for constant deep range.
The plan should balance matchup work with enough box snaps to use his best traits.
K: Early usage is simple: the leg has to travel, and the staff must decide whether the operation is repeatable under pressure.
The grade depends on whether the workout consistency matches the game-day temperament.
He needs stable mechanics and enough range to justify carrying a specialist through early misses.
P: Early usage depends on hang time, directional control, and whether the coverage unit can play off the ball flight.
The staff should value repeatable operation time as much as peak distance.
He needs to show the leg can flip field position without outkicking the coverage.
LS: Early usage is almost entirely trust based; one bad snap can matter more than a month of clean ones.
The evaluation should focus on operation time, placement, and protection reliability.
He has to be boring in the best possible way before a staff uses a roster spot.

high_end: The development plan should focus on turning a strong profile into weekly reliability, not rebuilding the player.
The main checkpoint is whether the details stay sharp once opponents can scheme against him.
He should be pushed quickly, but the staff still needs to protect the one area that could cap the ceiling.
The fastest path is a focused rookie role that expands once the floor proves stable.
This is more refinement than rescue, which is why the early investment can make sense.
The staff should have a year-one plan and a year-two expansion plan before turning in the card.
starter_path: The development plan should target two or three specific fixes instead of trying to remake everything at once.
He has enough tools to become a starter if the staff can clean up the repeatable errors.
The most important checkpoint is whether the shaky reps shrink by midseason of year one.
He needs a coaching staff willing to live with some early unevenness while the best traits mature.
The projection improves if he earns a narrow role first and then adds responsibility.
The staff should decide whether the flaws are habits, experience gaps, or true limitations.
developmental: The development plan should be specific, patient, and honest about what he cannot do yet.
He needs practice time and a protected package before the team asks for starter-level answers.
The staff should identify one carrying trait and build the first year around it.
The risk rises if the team drafts him without a clear teaching progression.
The best outcome probably requires a redshirt-style year with visible practice growth.
He needs the kind of coaching plan that turns flashes into habits.
roster_bubble: The development plan starts with survival: special teams, reliability, and one bankable trait.
He needs to make a staff notice him quickly because patience is limited at this part of the board.
The best path is a narrow job he can own before the roster squeeze arrives.
He has to become dependable in the unglamorous parts of the role before the upside matters.
The team should know exactly why it is keeping him by the end of camp.
He is more likely to stick if the staff gives him a simple weekly checklist.
high_risk: The development plan must be unusually clear because the tools alone will not carry the evaluation.
He needs a staff that can separate fixable issues from traits that may never stabilize.
The team should be comfortable with a wide range of outcomes before investing.
The projection can work, but only if the coaching staff is honest about the early limitations.
The first year may decide whether the ceiling is real or just a workout-grade mirage.
This is a conviction pick, and the conviction needs to come with a plan.

default: The area report leans on live exposure and how the player handled his week-to-week role.
This scout is weighting the player's best and worst tape without leaning too hard on one workout.
The opinion is shaped by usage context, competition level, and how often the role looked repeatable.
The report reads like a grounded area grade, with enough room for another scout to disagree.
national_scout: The national look is more comfortable separating the player from the college system.
This report puts extra weight on whether the profile translates across opponents and schemes.
The grade is less reactive to flashes and more focused on bankable NFL traits.
The national report is trying to avoid overcorrecting for one loud game or one quiet stretch.
optimistic_area: This is a favorable read that trusts the best tape more than the worst tape.
The scout may be giving extra credit for growth trajectory and daily exposure.
The report is willing to bet that the rough parts improve with NFL coaching.
The grade should be cross-checked because the optimism is doing some work.
conservative_crosscheck: The cross-check is intentionally stress-testing the miss case.
This report is less forgiving of traits that have not already become consistent production.
The grade may run colder than the room if the scout does not trust the projection.
The conservative read is useful because it asks what happens if the best trait does not travel.
traits_scout: The traits report is more willing to chase the athletic ceiling than the weekly floor.
This lens may push the player higher if the movement profile or body type is rare.
The grade is influenced by what the player could become, not only what he is right now.
The room should make sure the traits are tied to an actual football job.
analytics_scout: The analytical cross-check weighs age, production shape, role efficiency, and athletic context.
This report is asking whether the profile has evidence beyond traits and reputation.
The grade may punish older breakouts or players whose production depended heavily on scheme.
The lens is useful for catching prospects whose public profile is louder than the underlying indicators.
old_school_scout: The old-school read puts real weight on body type, toughness, and how the player looks getting off the bus.
This report can be valuable when the physical profile is obvious, but it may underplay skill variance.
The grade is more comfortable betting on size, speed, and temperament than a narrow spreadsheet case.
The room should check whether the physical tools actually show up in the key role traits.
medical_crosscheck: The medical cross-check is more sensitive to availability, durability, and workload projection.
This report may shade the grade down if the body history makes the role harder to trust.
The lens is less concerned with peak flashes than whether the player can stay available.
The grade should be paired with updated medical information before the final board stack.
small_school_scout: The small-school report is balancing clear dominance against the jump in weekly competition.
This scout is more willing to protect a late riser if the traits and practice exposure check out.
The grade carries extra uncertainty because the player may not have faced many NFL bodies.
The report should be cross-checked with all-star practices, combine work, and private visits.

variance_notes: This report is an opinion, not the true rating card.
Private workouts and interviews could move this report more than usual.
The grade is sensitive to how much the scout trusts the projection traits.
Another scout could reasonably stack him a tier higher or lower depending on role preference.
The final board should check whether the same strengths show up against better competition.
This is the kind of report that may age very differently after a year in an NFL building.
The workout data supports part of the opinion, but the tape still has to carry the grade.
The room should separate what the player already does from what the staff hopes to teach.
This note should be rechecked against interviews, medicals, and whether the role exists on the current roster.
The staff should ask whether this is a player evaluation or a fit evaluation before locking the grade.
There may be more confidence in the player than in the usage plan, which matters on draft weekend.
A late visit, pro day, or coaching fit could still change how the room stacks this file.
